annotations.label: Computational complexity theory

anchor.snippet: computational complexity theory focuses on classifying computational problems according to their resource usage

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of the field/discipline of computational complexity theory itself.

annotations.label: Computational problem

anchor.snippet: A computational problem is a task solved by a computer

annotations.note: There is no general definition of a 'computational problem' in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inherently difficult problem

anchor.snippet: A problem is regarded as inherently difficult if its solution requires significant resources

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of inherently difficult problems or resource lower bounds for problems.

annotations.label: Problem instance

anchor.snippet: A computational problem can be viewed as an infinite collection of instances

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define problems as collections of instances.

annotations.label: Primality testing instance

anchor.snippet: consider the problem of primality testing

annotations.note: Primality is formalized as a decidable predicate (`Nat.decidablePrime`), giving an algorithm to test instances, but not as a complexity-theory problem.

annotations.label: Travelling salesman instance

anchor.snippet: consider the following instance of the decision version of the travelling salesman problem

annotations.note: The travelling salesman problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Instance as string over an alphabet

anchor.snippet: a problem instance is a string over an alphabet

annotations.note: `Computability.Encoding` formalizes encoding a type as strings (`List Γ`) over an alphabet, which is the representation device, though not framed as 'problem instances'.

annotations.label: Decision problem

anchor.snippet: A decision problem is a type of computational problem where the answer is either yes or no

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Language α := Set (List α)` is exactly the formal-language view of a decision problem, but there is no decl named/defined as 'decision problem'.

annotations.label: Graph connectivity decision problem

anchor.snippet: An example of a decision problem is the following.

annotations.note: Graph connectivity is formalized as the predicate `SimpleGraph.Connected`, but not cast as a decision problem/language.

annotations.label: Function problem

anchor.snippet: A function problem is a computational problem where a single output

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define the complexity-theoretic notion of a function problem.

annotations.label: Multiplication recast as decision problem

anchor.snippet: the multiplication of two integers can be expressed as the set of triples

annotations.note: Integer multiplication exists, but its recasting as the decision-problem set of triples is not formalized.

annotations.label: Worst-case time complexity

anchor.section: Measuring the size of an instance
anchor.snippet: is defined to be the maximum time taken over all inputs of size

annotations.note: `TM2ComputableInTime` bounds steps by `time(input.length)` over all inputs of that length (worst case), but worst-case time complexity is not defined as a stand-alone quantity.

annotations.label: Polynomial time algorithm

anchor.section: Measuring the size of an instance
anchor.snippet: the algorithm is said to be a polynomial time algorithm

annotations.note: `TM2ComputableInPolyTime` packages a Turing machine with a polynomial time bound proving it computes a function in polynomial time.

annotations.label: Cobham's thesis

anchor.section: Measuring the size of an instance
anchor.snippet: Cobham's thesis argues that a problem can be solved with a feasible amount of resources if and only if it admits a polynomial-time algorithm

annotations.note: Cobham's thesis is an informal claim and is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Turing machine

anchor.snippet: A Turing machine is a mathematical model of a general computing machine.

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Turing machine models (TM0/TM1/TM2), e.g. `Turing.TM0.Machine`.

annotations.label: Church–Turing thesis

anchor.snippet: this is the statement of the Church–Turing thesis

annotations.note: The Church–Turing thesis is informal and not formalized.

annotations.label: Deterministic Turing machine

anchor.snippet: A deterministic Turing machine is the most basic Turing machine

annotations.note: Mathlib's Turing machines are deterministic by construction (`step : Cfg → Option Cfg`), so `Turing.TM0.Machine` is a deterministic Turing machine.

annotations.label: Probabilistic Turing machine

anchor.snippet: A probabilistic Turing machine is a deterministic Turing machine with an extra supply of random bits.

annotations.note: Probabilistic/randomized Turing machines are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-deterministic Turing machine

anchor.snippet: A non-deterministic Turing machine is a deterministic Turing machine with an added feature of non-determinism

annotations.note: Mathlib's Turing machines are deterministic only; nondeterministic Turing machines are not formalized.

annotations.label: Equivalence of deterministic machine models

anchor.snippet: each of these models can be converted to another without providing any extra computational power

annotations.note: `PartrecToTM2.tr_eval` shows a TM2 machine evaluates partial-recursive programs, establishing one model equivalence, but no general 'all reasonable models are equivalent' theorem is stated.

annotations.label: Time required by a Turing machine

anchor.snippet: is the total number of state transitions, or steps, the machine makes before it halts

annotations.note: `TM2OutputsInTime` counts the number of steps until halting, capturing running time, but as a step-count bound rather than a named time measure.

annotations.label: DTIME complexity class

anchor.snippet: is then denoted by DTIME

annotations.note: The DTIME complexity class is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Best, worst and average case complexity

anchor.section: Best, worst and average case complexity
anchor.snippet: refer to three different ways of measuring the time complexity

annotations.note: Best/average-case complexity measures (and the distinction) are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quicksort complexity

anchor.section: Best, worst and average case complexity
anchor.snippet: the deterministic sorting algorithm quicksort addresses the problem of sorting a list of integers

annotations.note: Quicksort and its complexity analysis are not in Mathlib (only merge sort exists, without complexity bounds).

annotations.label: Complexity class

anchor.snippet: A complexity class is a set of problems of related complexity.

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general notion of a complexity class.

annotations.label: Cobham–Edmonds thesis

anchor.snippet: the time complexities in any two reasonable and general models of computation are polynomially related

annotations.note: The Cobham–Edmonds thesis (polynomial relation of time across models) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Complexity class P

anchor.snippet: the complexity class P , which is the set of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine within polynomial time

annotations.note: Polynomial-time deterministic TM computation exists via `TM2ComputableInPolyTime`, but the class P (set of poly-time decision problems) is not defined.

annotations.label: Savitch's theorem (PSPACE = NPSPACE)

anchor.snippet: It turns out that PSPACE = NPSPACE and EXPSPACE = NEXPSPACE by Savitch's theorem.

annotations.note: Space complexity classes and Savitch's theorem are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Time hierarchy theorem

anchor.snippet: the time hierarchy theorem states that

annotations.note: The time hierarchy theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Space hierarchy theorem

anchor.snippet: The space hierarchy theorem states that

annotations.note: The space hierarchy theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: P strictly contained in EXPTIME

anchor.snippet: the time hierarchy theorem tells us that P is strictly contained in EXPTIME

annotations.note: The classes P and EXPTIME and the separation P ⊊ EXPTIME are not formalized.

annotations.label: Reduction

anchor.snippet: A reduction is a transformation of one problem into another problem.

annotations.note: `ManyOneReducible` formalizes computable many-one reduction between problems, but not the resource-bounded (e.g. polynomial-time) reductions used in complexity theory.

annotations.label: Squaring reduces to multiplication

anchor.snippet: the problem of squaring an integer can be reduced to the problem of multiplying two integers

annotations.note: This specific reduction between squaring and multiplication problems is not formalized.

annotations.label: Hard for a complexity class

anchor.snippet: is hard for a class of problems

annotations.note: Hardness for a complexity class is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complete for a complexity class

anchor.snippet: is said to be complete for

annotations.note: Completeness for a complexity class is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: P is contained in NP

anchor.snippet: each problem in P is also member of the class NP

annotations.note: The classes P and NP and the inclusion P ⊆ NP are not formalized.

annotations.label: Ladner's theorem (NP-intermediate problems)

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: It was shown by Ladner that if

annotations.note: Ladner's theorem on NP-intermediate problems is not formalized.

annotations.label: Graph isomorphism problem

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: The graph isomorphism problem is the computational problem of determining whether two finite graphs are isomorphic

annotations.note: Graph isomorphism is formalized as `SimpleGraph.Iso` (`G ≃g H`), but not as the computational decision problem.

annotations.label: Integer factorization problem

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: The integer factorization problem is the computational problem of determining the prime factorization of a given integer.

annotations.note: Prime factorization is formalized (`Nat.factorization`, `Nat.primeFactorsList`), but not framed as the integer-factorization computational problem.

annotations.label: co-NP (complement class)

anchor.snippet: is the class containing the complement problems

annotations.note: NP and its complement class co-NP are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intractable problem

anchor.snippet: is known as an intractable problem

annotations.note: The notion of an intractable problem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Tractable problem

anchor.snippet: a problem that can be solved in practice is called a tractable problem

annotations.note: The notion of a tractable problem is not formalized.

annotations.label: EXPTIME-hard problems are intractable

anchor.snippet: Problems that are known to be intractable in this sense include those that are EXPTIME

annotations.note: EXPTIME-hardness and intractability claims are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Presburger arithmetic not in P

anchor.snippet: the decision problem in Presburger arithmetic has been shown not to be in

annotations.note: Presburger arithmetic is formalized (`Language.presburger`), but its complexity lower bound (not in P) is not.

annotations.label: Exponential-time program runtime

anchor.snippet: consider a program that makes

annotations.note: This illustrative exponential-runtime example is not formalized in Mathlib.
